title: Evan Williams (Internet entrepreneur)

infobox.value: Evan Clark Williams   (1972-03-31) March 31, 1972 (age 53)[[1]](#cite_note-nytimes-1)  [Clarks, Nebraska](/wiki/Clarks,_Nebraska "Clarks, Nebraska"), U.S.

(born March 31, 1972) is an American [internet entrepreneur](/wiki/Internet_entrepreneur "Internet entrepreneur").[[2]](#cite_note-EvWimsLATimes2013-2) He is a co-founder of [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), and was its CEO from 2008 to 2010, and a member of its [board](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors") from 2007 to 2019.[[3]](#cite_note-Forbes_profile-3) He founded [Blogger](/wiki/Blogger_(service) "Blogger (service)") and [Medium](/wiki/Medium_(website) "Medium (website)"). In 2014, he co-founded the [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") firm Obvious Ventures. As of May 2025, his net worth is estimated at US$2 billion, according to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*.[[3]](#cite_note-Forbes_profile-3)

outgoing_links.target_title: Acquisition of Twitter by Elon Musk

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American entrepreneur (born 1972)

| Evan Williams | |
| --- | --- |
| Williams in 2014 | |
| Born | Evan Clark Williams   (1972-03-31) March 31, 1972 (age 53)[[1]](#cite_note-nytimes-1)  [Clarks, Nebraska](/wiki/Clarks,_Nebraska "Clarks, Nebraska"), U.S. |
| Education | [University of Nebraska–Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") (dropped out) |
| Occupation | Internet entrepreneur |
| Years active | 1993–present |
| Known for | * [Blogger](/wiki/Blogger_(service) "Blogger (service)") * [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") * [Medium](/wiki/Medium_(website) "Medium (website)") |
| Spouse | Sara Morishige Williams (divorced)[[1]](#cite_note-nytimes-1) |
| Children | 2 |

**Evan Clark Williams** (born March 31, 1972) is an American [internet entrepreneur](/wiki/Internet_entrepreneur "Internet entrepreneur").[[2]](#cite_note-EvWimsLATimes2013-2) He is a co-founder of [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), and was its CEO from 2008 to 2010, and a member of its [board](/wiki/Board_of_directors "Board of directors") from 2007 to 2019.[[3]](#cite_note-Forbes_profile-3) He founded [Blogger](/wiki/Blogger_(service) "Blogger (service)") and [Medium](/wiki/Medium_(website) "Medium (website)"). In 2014, he co-founded the [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") firm Obvious Ventures. As of May 2025, his net worth is estimated at US$2 billion, according to *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*.[[3]](#cite_note-Forbes_profile-3)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Williams was born in [Clarks, Nebraska](/wiki/Clarks,_Nebraska "Clarks, Nebraska"), as the third child of Laurie Howe and Monte Williams.[[4]](#cite_note-4) He grew up on a farm in Clarks, where he assisted with crop irrigation during the summers. He attended the [University of Nebraska–Lincoln](/wiki/University_of_Nebraska%E2%80%93Lincoln "University of Nebraska–Lincoln") for a year and a half, where he joined [FarmHouse](/wiki/FarmHouse "FarmHouse") fraternity, then left the school to pursue his career.[[5]](#cite_note-NYT_Ev-5)[[6]](#cite_note-WSJ_Twitter_Revolution-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early career")]

After departing from college, Williams took on various technology jobs and [start-up](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") firms in [Key West](/wiki/Key_West "Key West"), Florida, and in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") and [Austin](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"), Texas, before returning to his family farm in Nebraska. In 1996 Williams moved to [Sebastopol, California](/wiki/Sebastopol,_California "Sebastopol, California") in [Sonoma County](/wiki/Sonoma_County "Sonoma County") to work for the technology publishing company [O'Reilly Media](/wiki/O%27Reilly_Media "O'Reilly Media"). He started at O'Reilly in a marketing position, later becoming an independent contractor writing computer code, which led to freelance opportunities with companies including [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") and [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard").[[6]](#cite_note-WSJ_Twitter_Revolution-6) While working at O'Reilly, he also started a website called EvHead.com, where he first began blogging about his thoughts.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Pyra Labs and Blogger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Pyra Labs and Blogger")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/2001_webby_awards.jpg/250px-2001_webby_awards.jpg)](/wiki/File:2001_webby_awards.jpg)

Williams at [Webby Awards](/wiki/Webby_Awards "Webby Awards") in 2001

Williams and [Meg Hourihan](/wiki/Meg_Hourihan "Meg Hourihan") co-founded [Pyra Labs](/wiki/Pyra_Labs "Pyra Labs") to make project management software. A note-taking feature spun off as [Blogger](/wiki/Blogger_(service) "Blogger (service)"), one of the first web applications for creating and managing [weblogs](/wiki/Weblog "Weblog").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Williams coined the term "blogger" and was instrumental in the popularization of the term "blog".[[10]](#cite_note-10) Pyra survived the departure of Hourihan and other employees, and was later acquired by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") on February 13, 2003.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2003, Williams was named to the [MIT](/wiki/Massachusetts_Institute_of_Technology "Massachusetts Institute of Technology") [Technology Review](/wiki/Technology_Review "Technology Review") [TR100](/wiki/TR35 "TR35") as one of the top 100 innovators in the world under the age of 35.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 2004, he was named one of *[PC Magazine](/wiki/PC_Magazine "PC Magazine")'*s "People of the Year", along with Hourihan and Paul Bausch, for their work on Blogger.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Odeo and Obvious Corporation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Odeo and Obvious Corporation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Evan_Williams%2C_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg/250px-Evan_Williams%2C_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg)](/wiki/File:Evan_Williams,_Web_2.0_Conference.jpg)

Williams at [Web 2.0 Summit](/wiki/Web_2.0_Summit "Web 2.0 Summit") in 2005

Williams officially left Google on October 8, 2004,[[14]](#cite_note-14) to co-found [Odeo](/wiki/Odeo "Odeo"), a [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") company.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In late 2006, Williams co-founded [Obvious Corporation](/wiki/Obvious_Corporation "Obvious Corporation") with [Biz Stone](/wiki/Biz_Stone "Biz Stone") and other former Odeo employees, to acquire all previous properties from Odeo's former backers.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In April 2007, Odeo was acquired by Sonic Mountain.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Twitter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Twitter")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/TechCrunch50_Day_Two_Afternoon_-_Evan_Williams.jpg/250px-TechCrunch50_Day_Two_Afternoon_-_Evan_Williams.jpg)](/wiki/File:TechCrunch50_Day_Two_Afternoon_-_Evan_Williams.jpg)

Williams at [TechCrunch50](/wiki/TechCrunch50 "TechCrunch50") in 2008

Among Obvious Corporation's projects was [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), a popular, free social networking and micro-blogging service. Twitter was spun out as a new company in April 2007, with Williams as co-founder, board member, and investor.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In October 2008, Williams became CEO of Twitter, succeeding [Jack Dorsey](/wiki/Jack_Dorsey "Jack Dorsey"), who became chairman of the board.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

By February 2009, [Compete.com](/wiki/Compete.com "Compete.com") ranked Twitter the third most-used social network, based on their count of 6 million unique monthly visitors and 55 million monthly visits.[[20]](#cite_note-Compete-20) As of February 2013, Twitter had 200 million registered users.[[21]](#cite_note-21) It gets 300,000 new users a day and, as of August 2015, was ranked twelfth in the world. It receives more than 300 million unique visitors and more than five billion people in traffic a month. 75% of its traffic comes from outside of Twitter.com.

In October 2010, Williams stepped down from the CEO position, explaining that he would be "completely focused on product strategy," and appointed [Dick Costolo](/wiki/Dick_Costolo "Dick Costolo") as his replacement.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Following the announcement of Twitter's [initial public offering (IPO)](/wiki/Initial_public_offering "Initial public offering") in 2013, the company was valued at between US$14 billion and US$20 billion.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

On April 6, 2017, an article announced Williams would sell 30 percent of his stock in Twitter, for "personal reasons."[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In February 2019, Williams stepped down from his role as a board member of Twitter.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Medium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Medium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Web_Summit_2018_-_Press_Conference_-_Day_3%2C_November_8_SD5_9161_%2831913004358%29.jpg/250px-Web_Summit_2018_-_Press_Conference_-_Day_3%2C_November_8_SD5_9161_%2831913004358%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Web_Summit_2018_-_Press_Conference_-_Day_3,_November_8_SD5_9161_(31913004358).jpg)

Williams speaking at [2018 Web Summit](/wiki/Web_Summit "Web Summit")

On September 25, 2012, Williams created the publishing platform [Medium](/wiki/Medium_(website) "Medium (website)"). Initially, it was available only to early adopters, but was opened to the public in 2013.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

On April 5, 2013, Williams and Stone announced that they would be unwinding Obvious Corporation as they focused on individual startups.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On July 12, 2022, it was announced Williams would step down as Medium's CEO and become the chairman of the board.[[28]](#cite_note-EvWillMediumStepDownNYT2022-28)[[29]](#cite_note-tc-12jul2022-29)[[30]](#cite_note-medium-12jul2022-30)

### Obvious Ventures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Obvious Ventures")]

In 2014, Williams co-founded the venture capital firm Obvious Ventures with James Joaquin and Vishal Vasishth. The firm is focused on companies they believe can make a positive change in the world.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) Since its inception the fund has raised about $585 million—$123 million in its first round, $191 million in its second and $271 million in its latest round.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
In 2024, Williams announced his new company, Mozi, which is a private social network he co-founded with entrepreneur [Molly DeWolf Swenson](/wiki/Molly_DeWolf_Swenson "Molly DeWolf Swenson"). Mozi has raised $6M from investors including Obvious Ventures, WndrCo and BBG Ventures.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Williams lives in the [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco") area with his wife, Sara, with whom he has two children.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) He is a [vegetarian](/wiki/Vegetarian "Vegetarian"),[[6]](#cite_note-WSJ_Twitter_Revolution-6) and has invested – through the Obvious Corporation – in a [plant-based](/wiki/Plant-based "Plant-based") [meat alternative](/wiki/Meat_alternative "Meat alternative"), [Beyond Meat](/wiki/Beyond_Meat "Beyond Meat").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

## Views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Evan_Williams_(Internet_entrepreneur)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Views")]

Williams has been quoted as having a philosophy that it is important to conserve time, do fewer things, and to avoid distractions.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Ev-Williams-2013.jpg/250px-Ev-Williams-2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ev-Williams-2013.jpg)

Williams speaking at [XOXO Festival](/wiki/XOXO_Festival "XOXO Festival") in 2013

Williams presented at the 2013 [XOXO Festival](/wiki/XOXO_Festival "XOXO Festival") in [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), and explained his understanding of Internet commerce.[[39]](#cite_note-Wired-39) During his XOXO session, Williams also likened the Internet to "a lot of other major technological revolutions that have taken place in the history of the world," such as [agriculture](/wiki/First_Agricultural_Revolution "First Agricultural Revolution"), and asserted that the Internet is not a [utopia](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia").[[39]](#cite_note-Wired-39)

After President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") credited his election win to the use of Twitter, Evan Williams stated that if true, he was sorry.[[40]](#cite_note-chicagotribune-40) He also said that the internet is "obviously broken because it rewards extremes".[[40]](#cite_note-chicagotribune-40) Williams told the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") that he was wrong to think that an open platform where people could speak freely would make the world a better place.[[40]](#cite_note-chicagotribune-40) His musings about future business objectives include considerations about the effect of the Internet upon society.[[41]](#cite_note-41)
